vol_name: Classroom-Based Social Intervention for Spatial Skills
vol_description:
Spatial skills underlie how humans acquire, represent, organize, and navigate the environment; mentally manipulate objects; and communicate information about objects and environments to others. While educators have increasingly realized that early interventions in reading, writing, and arithmetic have cascading effects that lead to academic and professional success, an intentional practice of spatial skills is largely absent. We examined the value of training spatial skills as a group in a classroom setting with Reception age children.

Children at the Underhill school in London were trained in spatial skills once a week (session duration = 35 minutes) for six weeks in three groups: (1) “social” group: performed a unique, innovative group intervention in which subgroups played spatial games; (2) “individuals” group: performed an individual version of the spatial games; and (3) control group: participated in phonics acting activity. 

The current dataset includes each intervention session for the "social group".

vol_name: Testing Human Learning to Adapt using Altered Gravity in a Virtual Reality Environment
vol_description: A remarkable aspect of human behaviour is the ability to respond quickly to changes in local conditions. This adaptability is critical for survival and proper function in daily life because everyday environments are variable, unpredictable, and full of novel situations. Humans need to learn to modify even the most basic and automatised actions (e.g., reaching or walking2 to suit the current demands (e.g., object orientation or obstacles in the path). In the current study, we examine how humans learn to adapt to difference environments by testing them in a basketball-throwing game and altering the gravity in a virtual reality environment. Participants need to adapt their movements and throwing strategies to the altered gravity. Participants practice using training regimens that differ in the level of variability and sense of agency (active vs. observational learning).

vol_name: Automatic real-time hand tracking enhances adolescents’ spatial learning by eliminating haptic feedback
vol_description: Spatial skills underlie how humans acquire, represent, organise, manipulate, and navigate their environment, and therefore are fundamental for survival and proper function. Spatial skills require the integration of sensory-motor information with higher-order cognitive representations. Yet, the role of sensory modality in enhancing these skills through training is limited. Here, we tested a new application for real-time machine-learning-based hand tracking as a manipulator of sensory modality that enhances spatial skills in late childhood. To that end, child students (ages 14-15) completed an 8-session school training programme to improve their spatial skills. Children were randomly assigned into one of two training regimens: Hapto-Visual (HV; students constructed a physical 2D polydron net from different perspectives of a 3D cube) or Visual-Only (VO; students constructed a digitised version of the 2D plydron net without tactile information). The efficacy of the training was assessed using traditional spatial skill tests (Modified Mental Rotation and Mental Paper-Folding Tasks) administered before and after the training programme. We found significant improvement in both groups, with a significantly enhanced performance by the VO group compared to the HV group. Our findings suggest that omitting haptic feedback during spatial training compels reliance on mental representations, thereby bolstering spatial skills more effectively. These findings present a new application for real-time hand-tracking technology in educational settings and demonstrate its cost-effective potential to advance spatial abilities in young students.

vol_name: The BlockQuest Game: Digital Behavioral Phenotyping of ADHD During Embodied Problem Solving in Virtual Reality
vol_description:
Attention-deficit-hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) is a neurodevelopmental condition. Children with ADHD exhibit higher levels of inattention, hyperactivity, and impulsivity compared to their typically developing peers, impacting their daily functioning. To characterize the effects of ADHD, traditional research methodologies have predominantly used an outcome-oriented approach—scoring at which ages children solve particular problems compared to typically developing children. However, such an approach neglects how children solve tasks and overlooks the role of embodiment in everyday problems. That is, how perceptual, cognitive, and motor processes unfold from moment to moment during problem solving. Recent research attempted to address this knowledge gap using computerized gamification of tasks. However, most gamified paradigms are stationary and overlook the aspect of locomotion and embodiment in these tasks, which are strongly related to daily function. 

In this project, we test the effects of ADHD on daily living should be characterized through the use of virtual reality as a modernized embodied tool that provides a digital behavioral phenotyping of ADHD.  We present a novel embodied block-construction serious game paradigm in virtual reality in which we systematically capture the real-time interaction between perception, cognition, and movement.

vol_name: The Role of Real-Time Embodied Experience in Physical Reasoning in School-Aged Children
vol_description:
The ability to rapidly adapt to changes in the environment is critical to survival. With the rapid development of space exploration, adaptability skills will be essential to interact with extreme environments. Non-terrestrial gravity is indeed the ultimate frontier for adaptation, given its dramatic effects on the body and brain. Understanding how adaptation emerges will help to address these challenges.
This study uses natural altered gravity simulation in controlled lab settings to investigate how school-aged children learn to adapt their actions to environmental challenges. Specifically, we tested the effects of natural altered vestibular-gravitational signalling on reasoning and learning strategies. Healthy school-aged children completed a series of virtual physical reasoning games while their vestibular-gravitational signalling is altered by natural vestibular stimulation (by changing body orientation from upright to supine). Critically, the physics in the game was also be systematically modified in terms of terrestrial vs. non-terrestrial gravity.

vol_name: Variable Embodied Experience Improves Children’s Ability to Reason About Altered Physical Laws
vol_description:
A key component underlying children’s successful interactions with the external world is their ability to reason about future events. Previous studies showed that this reasoning skill is critical when humans need to adapt to real-world environments that are variable, unpredictable, and full of novel situations. 
Despite the importance of reasoning and adaptability to human function and survival, existing evidence is limited in explaining how and which embodied experience affects high-level reasoning when adaptability is required. Here, we investigated the effects of different types of embodied experiences on high-level reasoning using virtual-reality (VR) environments with altered gravities. We tested 82 school-aged children (8- to 12-year-olds) and adults in a basketball game where they were asked to throw a ball into a basket under different gravities. They were randomly split into three experimental groups in which the practice differs by the level of variability in gravity and distance inside a VR setting. Before and after the VR practice, all participants were tested in a computerized game which consisted of a series of physical reasoning challenges.
We found that more variable practice in the VR setting led to better adaptation of physical reasoning. In addition, different VR practices led to different strategies for how participants solved the reasoning problems. Finally, an interaction effect indicated that children were affected more by the VR practice compared to adults. Our results demonstrate developmental changes in the effects of real-time embodied experience on high-level reasoning and suggest reasoning can be improved with more variable embodied experience.
